front: Composer: worked with librettist Ranieri de' Calzabigi to eliminate da capo arias, secco recitatives, and melismas; principles set out in preface to Alceste; Wagner cited him as predecessor of the Gesamtkunstwerk
Composer: Handel said his cook knew more counterpoint than him; music teacher of Marie Antoinette; primary operatic model for Berlioz
Composer: rivalry with Niccolò Piccinni in Paris; both commissioned to set the same libretto; Parisians split into 'Gluckists' and Piccinni fans
Composer: suffered a stroke and had Salieri take over the composition of Les Danaïdes; Echo et Narcisse's failure caused retirement to Vienna

Opera: 1762 reform opera; contains the 'Dance of the Blessed Spirits' (added in 1774 French revision); most famous aria is 'Che farò senza Euridice'; lead role was sung by a castrato in Vienna premiere
Opera: in Act II, a dancer removes a scarf to reveal Elysium; Amore talks the protagonist out of suicide in 'Tendre amour' and brings Euridice back to life

Opera: preface/manifesto (with Calzabigi) decried 'tiresome ritornellos' and said an overture should 'apprise the spectator of the action'; title character sings 'Divinités du Styx' resolving to die for her husband
Opera: Berlioz argued Mozart plagiarized the Commendatore's music in Don Giovanni from this opera; Rousseau criticized its Italian version as monotonous
Opera: most developed reform opera; opens with calm passage leading directly into a storm (no overture); a scene where the orchestral accompaniment reveals a character is lying
Opera: the Paris Opera commissioned both Gluck and Niccolò Piccinni to write rival settings; the feud was driven by a split between the king's mistress and the dauphine
Opera: third reform opera with Calzabigi; features 'O del mio dolce ardor' (long misattributed to Stradella); Erasto turns out to be Cupid in disguise; Janet Baker's operatic farewell
